articles.title: These 80-Year-Olds Have Brains Like 50-Year-Olds!
articles.summary: Imagine being 80 years old but remembering things as well as someone who's only 50. Scientists call these amazing people 'SuperAgers.' For over 25 years, researchers at Northwestern University have studied 290 SuperAgers and 77 donated brains. They found two secrets: some SuperAgers' brains are 'resistant' (they don't build up bad proteins linked to Alzheimer's), and others are 'resilient' (they have those proteins but stay sharp anyway). SuperAgers also have bigger, healthier brain cells in memory areas and are super social. This proves that losing your memory isn't guaranteed when you get old—and scientists hope to use these clues to help everyone stay sharp.

articles.title: Butter vs. Olive Oil: Which Fat Is Actually Good for You?
articles.summary: The U.S. government just released a new food pyramid that says eat lots of butter, beef fat, and olive oil. But nutrition experts are pushing back! They say swapping animal fats (like butter) for plant-based fats (like olive and seed oils) is way better for your heart and can lower your risk of disease. The new guidelines were made by a different group than usual—and some of those people had ties to the meat and dairy industry. Experts worry that if kids eat more meat and butter, they'll eat fewer fruits, veggies, and grains, missing out on important fiber and nutrients. So what's a kid to do? Science says go for nuts, seeds, fish, and olive oil—and don't be afraid…

articles.title: Trees Glow During Storms! Scientists Finally Prove It
articles.summary: For over 70 years, scientists suspected that trees might glow with tiny bursts of electricity during thunderstorms—but nobody had ever seen it in nature. Until now! A team from Penn State drove a special van (with a telescope on the roof!) to North Carolina and pointed it at a sweetgum tree during a storm. They captured 859 tiny electrical sparks called 'corona discharges' coming from the tree's leaves. These sparks create a faint ultraviolet glow and also help clean the air by producing a chemical called hydroxyl, which breaks down pollutants. This is the first time corona discharges have been confirmed in a natural forest setting—proving that even in 2026, there are still amazing discoveries waiting to be made!
